title: A detailed unboxing of Skyrim: The Adventure Game and the From the Ashes expansion
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1_CE104JHi8

Daniel (@dungeondive) does a detailed unboxing of Modiphius's Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim — The Adventure Game (retail version, ~$120) plus the From the Ashes expansion. Like many, he's poured 500+ hours into the video game without finishing it, preferring to ignore the main quest and just live in the world — so the game's free-roam mode, which mixes all the event and chapter cards for open-ended questing, is the part he's most excited to try.

The box reveals a resettable legacy experience: two six-chapter campaigns (the Blades and the Civil War, the latter holding no appeal for him), dual-layer character boards, nice hefty custom dice, and a huge library of loot, enchantments, gear and spells. But his recurring complaint is presentation: a reportedly dreadful rulebook (the BGG forum is wall-to-wall rules questions), an "aggressively ugly" generic map, cards with microscopic text marooned in blank space, and a startling amount of empty air in a very expensive box. He suspects the license is most of the cost, and muses Modiphius might have done better building an original world. Cautiously hopeful, he's bracing to learn a difficult game.

----

Daniel guesses much of Skyrim's steep price is the licence, and wonders if Modiphius should have built an original world instead. Does a beloved IP make you forgive a game's flaws, or raise your expectations?
